nativeRange.habitat: Found on reef slopes and in lagoons, often in turbid coastal environments. Wild populations are generally considered uncommon.

decorPreferences: lower rockwork or sand bed placement
open space away from neighboring corals

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Weekly target feeding of micro-plankton, zooplankton, or small frozen foods (such as brine shrimp or copepods) is generally advised to support long-term health and growth.

goodWith: clownfish (may host in absence of anemone)
avoidWith: aggressive sweeper-tentacle corals placed in close proximity
fish known to nip at coral polyps
hammer or torch corals without adequate spacing

imageCandidates.notes: Ryan Boren; 2816x2112. Aquarium colony with fully extended salmon-pink polyps showing the classic 12-tentacle daisy/flower structure with white radiating tips; whole colony in frame, sharp, vibrant, the exact hobbyist look.

Jesse Hofmann-Smith; 2000x1360. Alveopora spongiosa in an aquarium under actinic/blue reef lighting, deep-red bodies with neon-green fluorescent flower polyps; showcases the glowing effect described in careNotes.
Ryan McMinds; 2048x1536. Alveopora allingi, whole rounded in-situ colony with dense extended brown polyps giving an anemone-like presence; sharp reef habitat shot, adds a distinct species.
Ewout Knoester; 2048x1536. In-situ Alveopora colony with pale blue-white tipped daisy polyps over brown corallites; whole colony in frame, crisp, shows a different colour form.

Roger Rittmaster; 2048x1638. Tight, sharp close-up of cream Alveopora polyps that clearly resolves the radiating flower/daisy tentacle pattern; diagnostic-feature detail shot (crop, so not recommended).

summary: Alveopora Coral is a large polyp stony coral with distinctive 12-tentacled, flower-like polyps and a light, porous skeleton. Closely related to Goniopora, it is generally considered the hardier of the two in captivity and a more accessible choice for intermediate reef keepers.
careNotes:
Alveopora encompasses approximately 17 recognized species, all belonging to the family Acroporidae. Despite its soft, flowing appearance in the aquarium, it builds a hard calcium carbonate skeleton — the defining characteristic of a stony coral — with a notably light and porous structure composed of interconnecting rods and spines. Polyps are large and fleshy with swollen, knob-like tentacle tips, and colonies may grow in massive rounded forms or branching arrangements.

Identification vs. Goniopora: Alveopora and Goniopora are frequently confused in the aquarium trade due to their very similar appearance — both are sometimes called 'Flowerpot Coral.' The key distinction is tentacle count: Alveopora polyps have 12 tentacles each, while Goniopora polyps have 24. Alveopora is generally considered hardier and more forgiving in captivity than Goniopora, making it the more accessible choice for intermediate reef keepers who want the flowerpot look without the added difficulty.

Placement and flow: Alveopora is generally advised to be placed near the bottom of the aquarium, on or near the sand bed, in low, indirect water movement. Direct, laminar flow tends to cause polyp retraction. When healthy and conditions are favorable, the polyps will extend dramatically, giving the colony an impressive, anemone-like presence.

Lighting: Moderate light intensity is generally recommended, in the range of 75–150 PAR. Alveopora has been observed to display a striking glowing effect under actinic (blue) lighting. Gradual light acclimation is advised when introducing new specimens.

Water chemistry: Regular supplementation with calcium, alkalinity, and magnesium is important for skeletal growth. Standard reef parameters of calcium 380–450 ppm, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, and magnesium 1250–1450 ppm are commonly recommended. Strontium and trace elements have also been noted as beneficial.

Aggression: Alveopora is generally not considered an aggressive coral, but fully extended polyps can reach and sting neighboring corals. Maintaining adequate spacing from neighbors — at least 10–15 cm — is advised.

Clownfish association: Clownfish have been observed hosting in Alveopora colonies in the absence of an anemone, though this behavior is not obligate and the coral is generally reported to tolerate it.
